content:
# 1 John Chapter 5 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1JN_003_page_001.jpg, 1JN_005_page_001.jpg

1. Everyone who believes that Jesus is the Christ has been born of God, and everyone who loves the Father loves whoever has been born of him.

2. By this we know that we love the children of God, when we love God and obey his commandments.

3. For this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments. And his commandments are not burdensome.

4. For everyone who has been born of God overcomes the world. And this is the victory that has overcome the world — our faith.

5. Who is it that overcomes the world except the one who believes that Jesus is the Son of God?

6. This is he who came by water and blood — Jesus Christ; not by the water only but by the water and the blood. And the Spirit is the one who testifies, because the Spirit is the truth.

7. For there are three that testify:

8. the Spirit and the water and the blood; and these three agree.

9. If we receive the testimony of men, the testimony of God is greater, for this is the testimony of God that he has borne concerning his Son.

10. Whoever believes in the Son of God has the testimony in himself. Whoever does not believe God has made him a liar, because he has not believed in the testimony that God has borne concerning his Son.

11. And this is the testimony, that God gave us eternal life, and this life is in his Son.

12. Whoever has the Son has life; whoever does not have the Son of God does not have life.

13. I write these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God, that you may know that you have eternal life.

14. And this is the confidence that we have toward him, that if we ask anything according to his will he hears us.

15. And if we know that he hears us in whatever we ask, we know that we have the requests that we have asked of him.

16. If anyone sees his brother committing a sin not leading to death, he shall ask, and God will give him life — to those who commit sins that do not lead to death. There is sin that leads to death; I do not say that one should pray for that.

17. All wrongdoing is sin, but there is sin that does not lead to death.

18. We know that everyone who has been born of God does not keep on sinning, but he who was born of God protects him, and the evil one does not touch him.

19. We know that we are from God, and the whole world lies in the power of the evil one.

20. And we know that the Son of God has come and has given us understanding, so that we may know him who is true; and we are in him who is true, in his Son Jesus Christ. He is the true God and eternal life.

21. Little children, keep yourselves from idols.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 5 is the letter's conclusion and summation. The "three witnesses" passage (vv.6-8) is textually significant: Sinaiticus does not contain the Comma Johanneum (the Trinitarian expansion found in late Latin manuscripts). The chapter moves from faith's victory (vv.1-5) through testimony (vv.6-12) to assurance (vv.13-21). The abrupt ending — "keep yourselves from idols" — is the letter's final punch: after all this theology, the practical command is blunt.

**Verse 20 — "He is the true God and eternal life":** The letter's christological climax. The grammatical ambiguity of houtos ("this one") creates a deliberate identification between the Father ("him who is true") and the Son ("his Son Jesus Christ"). Whether houtos refers to Christ or the Father, the author's point is the inseparability of the two.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.6 — "came by water and blood" vs. "was revealed by his baptism in water and by shedding his blood on the cross"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] having come through water and blood" | ho elthōn di' hydatos kai haimatos |
| LOGOS | "he who came by water and blood" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "was revealed as God's Son by his baptism in water and by shedding his blood on the cross" | Interpretive expansion: adds "baptism," "shedding," "on the cross" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author says "water and blood" — two words, deliberately compressed. PNEUMA expands this into a full narrative: "baptism in water" and "shedding his blood on the cross." While the interpretation (baptism and crucifixion) is widely accepted, the author's compressed phrasing is deliberate. The very brevity forces the reader to think about what "water and blood" mean — and scholars still debate whether water refers to baptism, birth (John 3:5), or the water from Jesus' side (John 19:34).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA expands the compressed into the explicit.

---

### v.19 — "the whole world lies in the power of the evil one" vs. "the world around us is under the control of the evil one"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the whole world lies in the evil one" | ho kosmos holos en tō ponērō keitai |
| LOGOS | "the whole world lies in the power of the evil one" | Adds "power" for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "the world around us is under the control of the evil one" | "Around us" added; "control" for keitai |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says the world "lies in" (keitai en) the evil one — a spatial metaphor of rest or immersion. LOGOS adds "power" to clarify the meaning (lying in the evil one's power). PNEUMA's "around us" is not in the text and "control" replaces the more passive "lies in" with active domination. The author's image is of the world passively resting in the evil one's sphere — not actively controlled by him.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.21 — "keep yourselves from idols" vs. "keep away from anything that might take God's place in your hearts"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "guard yourselves from idols" | phylaxate heauta apo tōn eidōlōn |
| LOGOS | "keep yourselves from idols" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "keep away from anything that might take God's place in your hearts" | Interprets "idols" broadly |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author says eidōla — "idols." The word means images, false gods, or objects of false worship. PNEUMA's expansive rendering ("anything that might take God's place in your hearts") is a valid application but not a translation. The author's bluntness is the point: after five chapters of nuanced theology, the final word is the simplest possible: no idols. Expanding it dilutes the impact.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA interprets rather than translates.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA expands compressed language | 1 | Interpretive expansion of "water and blood" (v.6) |
| PNEUMA adds spatial/interpretive glosses | 1 | "Around us" and "control" (v.19) |
| PNEUMA interprets rather than translates | 1 | "Anything that might take God's place" for "idols" (v.21) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 5's most significant disagreement is v.6 — the expansion of "water and blood" into explicit baptism and crucifixion references. The author's compressed language is deliberately open to multiple interpretations, and PNEUMA closes this openness. The final verse (v.21) illustrates the broader pattern: PNEUMA interprets where the author states, losing the rhetorical force of directness.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
